claims.claim: The battery module without thermal insulation had an average thermal spreading time of only 44.6 s per cell and a complete thermal spreading time of 134 s for the module.
claims.quote: the thermal spreading of the battery module (No.1 experiment) without insulation was very fast, with an average spreading time D of only 44.6 s for each cell, and a complete thermal spreading time D of 134 s for the module.

claims.claim: Using sponge ceramic nanofiber insulation increased the average thermal spreading time per cell by 5.27 times compared to the module without insulation, while nano-glass fiber insulation increased it by 7.36 times.
claims.quote: The average thermal spread time of the module with sponge ceramic insulation (No.2 experiment) increased by 5.27 times compared to the module without insulation. The average thermal spread time of the module with nano-glass fiber insulation (No.3 experiment) increased by 7.36 times compared to the module without insulation.

claims.claim: Nanofiber insulation layers slow down thermal transfer between cells but cannot reduce it sufficiently to prevent thermal runaway in neighboring cells, so the zero-spreading effect cannot be achieved.
claims.quote: the nanofiber insulation layer in the module only slows down the thermal transfer. But it is not able to reduce the thermal transfer between the two cells. Eventually, the temperature of the neighboring non-runaway cell will still continue to rise, and thermal runaway will eventually occur.

claims.claim: The average spreading time per cell with ceramic aerogel insulation was 756 s and with pre-oxidized silk aerogel was 1045 s, representing increases of 102% and 180% respectively compared to nano-glass fiber insulation.
claims.quote: The average spreading time of each battery was 756 s and 1045 s. And the complete thermal spreading time of the whole module was 2269 s and 3137 s. The average spreading time of the module with ceramic aerogel insulation (No. 4 experiment) increased by 102 % compared to the module with nanofiber glass insulation (No. 3 experiment). The average spreading time of the module with pre-oxidized filament-reinforced aerogel insulation (No. 5 experiment) increased by 180 % compared to the module with nanofiber glass insulation (No. 3 experiment).

claims.claim: Using composite phase change insulation (sponge ceramic + PCM or nano glass fiber + PCM) extended the thermal spreading interval between the first and second cells to an infinite length, achieving zero spreading of thermal runaway in the battery module.
claims.quote: In the battery module with sponge ceramic + PCM or nano-glass fiber + PCM composite phase change thermal insulation layer, the thermal spread interval D1, 2 between the first and second batteries was extended to an infinite length. Except for the first battery, no thermal runaway occurred in the other batteries. That is, the zero-spreading effect of thermal runaway of the battery module was achieved.

claims.claim: A convective heat transfer coefficient exceeding 40 W/m2-K is capable of entirely suppressing thermal runaway propagation in densely packed batteries under 2C-rate discharge conditions.
claims.quote: the convective environment with a heat dissipation coefficient exceeding 40 W/m2-K (corresponding to a velocity of 1 m/s in this study) can entirely suppress TRP

claims.claim: Increasing ambient pressure from 1 bar to 2 bar slowed thermal runaway propagation time from 634 s to 670 s, and completely inhibited propagation at 3 bar, under 3C discharge rate and 0.5 m/s flow speed.
claims.quote: the TRP time slowed from 634 s at 1 bar to 670 s at 2 bar, and was completely inhibited at a pressure of 3 bar. This is attributed to the increase in fluid density with ambient pressure, leading to higher heat transfer capability.

claims.claim: Nitrogen exhibited superior heat dissipation capabilities compared to air and argon gas, resulting in lower risk of thermal runaway propagation, while argon exhibited the lowest effectiveness due to its lower heat capacity.
claims.quote: the cooling system using argon gas exhibited the lowest effectiveness compared to air and nitrogen, primarily due to its lower heat capacity. Despite argon gas having higher density, its lower heat capacity exerts a more significant influence on the cooling rate. Nitrogen, on the other hand, exhibits superior capabilities in reducing the RTRP.

claims.claim: SEI decomposition begins at 74°C and serves as the initial trigger for thermal runaway; if this heat is dissipated before initiating subsequent self-heating reactions, the battery is less susceptible to thermal runaway even under abusive conditions.
claims.quote: The simulation results indicate that the heat generated by SEI decomposition, occurring at 74°C, serves as the initial trigger for TR. If this heat is dissipated to the surroundings before initiating the self-heating process through subsequent reactions, the battery is less susceptible to TR, even under abusive conditions.

claims.claim: When RTRP falls below 70%, thermal runaway cannot propagate to adjacent cells; it is recommended to keep RTRP below 50% for high-safety design requirements.
claims.quote: RTRP = 100 % characterizes the occurrence of flames throughout the entire battery module, and when RTRP < 70 %, there is no propagation of flames to neighboring cells. However, it is preferable for RTRP to remain below 50 % for enhanced battery module safety.

claims.claim: When the temperature exceeds 120°C, the melamine-urea-formaldehyde resin shell of the hydrated microcapsule ruptures and releases the encapsulated Ba(OH)2·8H2O core material.
claims.quote: Once the temperature exceeds 120 ◦C, the organic shell ruptures and releases the encapsulated core material.

claims.claim: Using 4 g/Ah of the Ba(OH)2·8H2O microcapsules reduces the total reaction enthalpy of full battery components by 24.61% as measured by DSC.
claims.quote: Differential scanning calorimetry results indicate that only 4 g/Ah of the microcapsules can reduce the total reaction enthalpy by 24.61 %.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature during thermal runaway was reduced by 106.2°C when 4 g/Ah of Ba(OH)2·8H2O microcapsules were incorporated into NCM811/Gr pouch cells, as measured by ARC tests.
claims.quote: Accelerating rate calorimeter tests indicate that the maximum temperature during thermal runaway has been reduced by 106.2 ◦C.

claims.claim: Increasing microcapsule dosage from 0 g/Ah to 6 g/Ah progressively reduced maximum thermal runaway temperature, but with diminishing returns; a marginal decrease of only 10.1°C was observed from 4 g/Ah to 6 g/Ah.
claims.quote: However, only a marginal decrease of 10.1 ◦C was observed from 4 g/Ah to 6 g/Ah, suggesting diminishing returns.

claims.claim: Increasing microcapsule dosage to 6 g/Ah unexpectedly reduced the thermal runaway trigger time to 1777.8 s compared to 2343.4 s at 4 g/Ah, attributed to excessive microcapsule content accelerating low-temperature side reactions near 120°C involving lithiated graphite.
claims.quote: However, increasing the dosage to 6 g/Ah unexpectedly reduced the trigger time to 1777.8 s. This phenomenon is attributed to excessive microcapsule content accelerating low-temperature side reactions near 120 ◦C, such as those involving lithiated graphite.

claims.claim: Direct addition of Ba(OH)2·8H2O without encapsulation caused irreversible capacity fading within the first 5 cycles, with capacity retention dropping to 5.56% by the 9th cycle, rendering the battery unusable.
claims.quote: The results showed irreversible capacity fading within the first 5 cycles, with the capacity retention rate dropping to 5.56 % by the 9th cycle, at which point the battery was deemed unusable.

claims.claim: The presence of hydrated microcapsules (An+Mp) reduced the reaction enthalpy of 100% SOC lithiated graphite anode from 648.32 J/g to 453.17 J/g, a decrease of 30.1%, by reacting water released from microcapsules with lithiated graphite.
claims.quote: in the presence of hydrated microcapsules (An+Mp), the heat generation of the graphite anode is significantly reduced, with the reaction enthalpy being 453.17 J/g, a decrease of 30.1 %.

claims.claim: After adding hydrated microcapsules to the anode-electrolyte mixture, no significant exothermic peaks appeared after 200°C, indicating that consumption of lithiated graphite by water from microcapsules significantly weakened subsequent reactions between lithiated graphite and electrolyte or binder.
claims.quote: no significant exothermic peaks appear after 200 ◦C. This indicates that the addition of hydrated microcapsules effectively modulates the sequence of thermal runaway reactions, consuming reactants through low-heat-generation reactions in advance.

claims.claim: Under lateral heating abuse conditions, the addition of microcapsules reduced the maximum temperature rise rate from 390°C/s to 52.75°C/s, a reduction of 86.47%.
claims.quote: Under the lateral heating experimental condition, the maximum temperature rise rate of the base sample was 390 ◦Cs(cid:0) 1, while that of the battery with microcapsules added was 52.75 ◦Cs(cid:0) 1, a reduction of 86.47 %.

claims.claim: The battery assembled with microcapsules achieved a capacity retention rate of 97.33% after 225 cycles at a charge rate of 1/3C and discharge rate of 1C, compared to 97.68% for the conventional battery.
claims.quote: after 255 cycles of charging at 1/3C rate and discharging at 1C rate, the discharge curves of the conventional battery and the battery with implanted hydrated microcapsules almost coincide. The capacity retention rate of the conventional battery is 97.68 %, while that of the battery with implanted hydrated microcapsules is 97.33 %.
